content:
First, set up  
 q(n)=p(5n),  
so q is a cubic in n with integer “scaled’’ coefficients.  The given condition p(5)+p(25)=1906 becomes  
 q(1)+q(5)=1906.  
We want to make |p(15)|=|q(3)| as small as possible.

1.  Find a particular “small’’ solution.  
   Take q(n)=Ln+M (a linear polynomial).  Then  
 q(1)=L+M, q(5)=5L+M,  
and  
 q(1)+q(5)=6L+2M=1906  ⇒ 3L+M=953.  
   Hence q(3)=3L+M=953.

2.  The general cubic q satisfying q(1)+q(5)=1906 is  
 q(n)=Ln+M + (n−1)(n−5)(kn+m).  
   At n=1 or 5 the second term vanishes, so q(1)+q(5) is still 1906.  
   Then  
 q(3)=953 −4(3k+m).  

3.  But because p has integer coefficients, one checks that k must be a multiple of 125 and m a multiple of 25, say  
 k=125K,  m=25M,  
so that  
 3k+m =375K+25M =25⋅T,  T∈ℤ.  
   Thus  
 q(3)=953 −4⋅25T =953 −100T.  

4.  To minimize |q(3)|=|953−100T| over integers T, choose T=10 to get  
 q(3)=953−1000=−47,  
and |q(3)|=47.  One checks no smaller absolute value is possible.

Hence the minimum possible value of |p(15)| is 47, and the answer is  
\boxed{47}.
